speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 7 of the Survivors of the Shoah interview with Moshe Taube. March 7, 1996. How were the guards in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards were very correct, I would say. Some were very correct, because they knew that their end was coming. But some were very bitter and hateful. You see? So you cannot have any uniform opinion of the guards. Some were OK, and some were still very, very hateful and-- and make people suffer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see them do that to people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. They were hitting people. Some were hitting people, and some were-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] some were very bitter and very, I would say, high-handed, heavy-handed. They make people do-- do things that-- over time. And-- and-- and-- and-- they make people feel like dirt, some of-- some of-- some of the guards. But some were more or less seeing the end, their end. So they were more amenable to-- to-- to-- to be-- to be-- be less stringent on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe how things started to come to a close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We saw [LAUGHS] American bombers over the-- over Brunnlitz once or twice in-- in-- in April of '45. And we were praying, drop some bombs already. We were praying with, you know, crying. Let-- let's hear some bombs already. But they were going to-- to-- to-- to different locations, to different targets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you ever leave Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brunnlitz-- Brunnlitz was not their target.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever leave Brunnlitz in this-- at all when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, not at all. This was all closed-- a-- a closed factory, a closed camp, enclosure. And then in May 8, 1945, all of a sudden, the camp was closed. The guards have disappeared. We knew that they ran away. At that time, I was very sick with my body illness, my boils and so on. And I heard people say, this is the end. They ran away. And all of a sudden, I saw some of the stronger men, so-called strong men in the camp, Jews, going out with rifles on their backs, on their shoulders. And later on, I learned that a Czech underground provided rifles into the camp so in case, god forbid, they will want us-- to-- to shoot us or-- before they left, there should be some kind of a resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contract with the underground when you were in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. yes-- not I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, you.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there were-- no, no. I didn't have any contact. But there were some elements and, you know, some people in our camp that established a contact with the Czech underground. The Czech underground was a communist underground, of course, and I don't know their strength. I don't know how strong they were. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On May 9, 1945, we saw a Russian soldier with a beard on a-- on a horse riding into the camp, and he started speak Yiddish. And he said, I am a member of the-- of the Red Army, and we are here. And he was mobbed by the inmates, and he was kissed and thrown into the air. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was the beginning of our liberation. Later on, the Russians came in force, and they brought bread into the camp, bread, holy bread into the camp, coveted bread into the camp, dreamed-about bread. And everybody was able to-- to eat to his satisfaction or her satisfaction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see Schindler at the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before he left, did you see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before he left, he gathered everybody, and he spoke to everyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, you will be remembered as the Schindler Juden. You will be remembered as the Schindler Jews. And everybody was clapping, and everybody was [SIGHS] emotional about it. But he left because he didn't want to fall into the Russian hands. So he left towards the West to be overtaken by the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the only time he ever spoke to the whole group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was the only time that he addressed it, the whole group of inmates, together, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any of the guards at the end also later on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They disappeared, because if they would be there I'm sure that they would be lynched. There's no question about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that happen after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw it-- it happen to a German inmate, to a German concentration camp inmate who came to Brunnlitz to serve as a-- as a sort of a head of-- of-- overseer-like of the-- of the inmates, so everybody should keep in discipline. He was a-- he was a murderer, a renowned murderer. This is why he was in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, but he was a German. After-- after when-- after the guards left, he was-- he was put to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was put to death by the-- by the edict of-- when the Russians came, he said, what are you-- why are you keeping him? Hang him. And he was hanged.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I went together with my father back to Cracow to look for-- to look for survivors of my family. And since I did not find any survivors, I went to Craiova, C-R-A-I-O-V-A, Craiova, in Romania, where my father's brother was-- lived after he escaped in '39 on a bike from Poland to Romania--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to save himself. Leopold Taube, [? [HEBREW] ?] Taube. He's still living in-- in Israel, and he's now 83, living still in Israel. So in Craiova in Romania, I was for one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: From when to when?

speaker: interviewee
text: From [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] June 1945 until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] June '46. At that time, I was-- I went on an illegal, so-called illegal, immigration to Eretz Israel, to Palestine at that time. I left together with a group of young Jews. I left Romania through Yugoslavia. And we were caught by the English Navy before Haifa, before we disembarked, and we were put into a kid camp for three months, where we had to wait our quota to be released and--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in November 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father went back to Poland. From Romania, he went back to Poland, where he met his second wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Hannah, Chanka, Schneeweis, S-C-H-N-E-E-W-E-I-S, Schneeweis. He married her, and he had two sons with her, twins. One of the twins, from you see there, was killed in the Yom Kippur War as a captain in the Israeli artillery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did he end up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your father end up?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father went to Israel in 1957, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he lived there until he died in 1990-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1991 he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in Israel, I learned cantorial. I-- I-- I mean, I completed my studies and my background in cantorial music and cantorial art in Israel. I, of course, I served in the Israeli Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see action?

speaker: interviewee
text: I served-- I served in Haganah first and then the Israeli Army. I saw action in the defense of Jerusalem in 1947, '48. And then I was released from the army, and I worked in a government office. But I was also a cantor in Haifa first, then in Tel Aviv, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until in-- in late 1957, I came to United States. And I was engaged by a congregation, Shaare Zedek in New York City, where I served for eight years until I came to Pittsburgh in 1965, 31 years ago. And I'm serving still this congregation, Beth Shalom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you go back to your hometown after the war, after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went on a mission to Poland when I was sent by the Association for the Advancement of Cantorial Arts based in Miami. They send me to Poland for concerts. There was a festival. And I took the occasion, and I had the opportunity to visit Cracow at that time. And I visited [? Dietla ?] 29 where I was-- the house where I was born and the-- and the room where- where I was born. There are now Poles living there, a lady with-- with her son, and they received me very nicely. I told them that I was born here, so they were in awe. And then I took my wife to Poland to show her the town of my birth, the city, and where my family lived, or they did, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married first?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was first married in 1949 in Jerusalem. My marriage last for 30 years, 31 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first wife's name was Bertha, but it did not work out. The marriage did not work out. And unfortunately, I had to terminate-- we had to terminate the marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Klipper, K-L-I-P-P-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have three children. I had three children with her, two daughters, and one son. The two daughters live in Israel, and one son lives in-- in Boston, next-- close to Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have [INAUDIBLE] over 16 grandchildren. And as a matter of fact, one of my-- my oldest grandchild is being mobilized to the Israeli Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remarried?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You remarried?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remarried married in 1983, and my present wife is Helena Taube. We are married already for 12 years, 13 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name is Gontcharow, G-O-N-T-R-- I'm sorry, G-O-N-T-C-H-A-R-O-W, Gontcharow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have a contact with Schindler after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father had, but not me. Schindler used to visit Israel almost every year to replenish his coffers, because he, you know, he was known to imbibe a lot, and his funds dried out many times. So there were Jews, survivors, Schindler survivors, in Israel, who provided him with funds every-- every time he came. That included my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever approached?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. My father did it for me [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- did you change your faith at all from the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: My faith? No, on the contrary. My faith got strengthened by the grace of God and by the fulfillment of the benediction of the blessing that I received from the holy rabbi, [? from ?] the [SPEAKING HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a message?

speaker: interviewee
text: My message is that it is incumbent upon the Jewish people [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to live in a way to merit [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] God's care, to preserve the mitzvot, to observe the mitzvot of the Torah, to live by Torah. Because this is the only way that the Jewish existence and Jewish continuity can be assured. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On a personal level, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my message is that everyone, every Jewish man, woman, child, has a destiny, a destiny that is fulfilled [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] by God's grace, a destiny that one can alter even for the good by good deeds. [SPEAKING HEBREW], by repentance, by prayer, and by charity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you introduce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my wife, [HEBREW], Helena Taube, to whom I am married almost 13 years, happily, fruitfully, and hopefully enduringly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd like to say it's been a wonderful life with my husband. And I'm sure he needed to say what he had to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does he talk about his experiences with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that much, no. It's too hard, too hard to relive everything, so.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she has plenty of information and plenty of recount to understand and to see where I come from, what I lived through. And she has-- she has enough-- enough of input in it so that our life is well-- I would say, well-based on memories, not only on memories of my past, but we have also memories, common memories, of our-- in our marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that we do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [? Got ?] it also quickly. It literally takes us 20 seconds each one.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather, Feivel Taube, and his wife, my grandmother, Golda Taube. This picture is from Krynica in Poland. It was taken in 1937. And I got it from my cousin, who is now in Hartford, Connecticut.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me when I was 10 years old and my sister when she was seven years old. It is in Cracow. And this picture was given to me by my uncle, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in-- when I was 10 years old, 1937. This is my bar mitzvah picture. It was taken when I was 13 years old in 1940. This is my mother and my sister. I got this picture from my uncle in Romania. This is a-- a class picture from 1936 when I was eight years old at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got this picture from my classmate who lives now in England, a newfound classmate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people from this picture survived the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can only remember one, this one, the one who presented me the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name, Moshe Richter. His name is Moshe Richter, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my father and myself when he came for a visit to New York in 1963 for the bar mitzvah of my son Ben Tzion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother of blessed memory, [HEBREW], Arie Taube, a captain in the Israeli Army in the artillery. He was killed in the Yom Kippur War on the Golan Heights in 1973.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1972.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was 24 when he was killed. These are my two daughters, Regina, [HEBREW], and Nina. This picture was taken when my daughter Regina was married to [? Mark ?] Berger in 1976.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Pittsburgh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. On time. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to run. I am sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, thank you. I'm sorry it went like this. But it was a good interview.
